content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES CoMM1ss10N 
-1-28 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Jonady Hum Sun 
Staff Counsel 
Public Utilities Commission 
505 Van Ness Avenue 
San Francisco, CA 94102-3298 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our file No. A-11-110 
Dear Mr. Sun: 
July 6, 2010 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
QUESTION 
Is the amount paid to a former employee for a class action settlement approved in 2011 
for overtime worked in 2005-2006 exempt from the definition of "income" under the Act? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. Regulation 18703.3 exempts from the definition of "income" wages paid by a 
former employer that accrued before the public official became a public official if there was no 
expectation of renewed employment. 
FACTS 
You are an attorney with the California Public Utilities Commission (the "CPUC") and 
you represent Ana Maria Johnson, a CPUC employee. Ms. Johnson worked for AT&T from 
August 2000- February 2008. In 2005-2006, Ms. Johnson accrued, but did not receive overtime 
wages. In April of 2011, Ms. Johnson received a payout of overtime wages that was part of an 
approved settlement in response to a class action lawsuit. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-11-110 
Page No. 2 
Ms. Johnson applied for a position with the CPUC in Febmary of 2011; she was hired 
and began working in March of that year. You now ask whether the overtime wages, paid in 
2011, but earned in 2005-2006, are "income" under the Act. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act states that: 
"a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a 
member of his or her immediate family, or on . . .  
[ ... ] 
"(c) any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending 
institution made in the regular course of business on terms available to the 
public without regard to official status, aggregating five hundred dollars or 
more in a value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made." 
(Section 87103) 
Regulation 18703.3(b), however, exempts from this definition income from former 
employers under the following circumstances: 
"Source of income, as used in Section 87103(c) and this section, shall not 
include a former employer if: All income from the employer was received 
by or accmed to the public official prior to the time he or she became a 
public official; the income was received in the normal course of the 
previous employment; and there was no expectation by the public official 
at the time he or she assumed office of renewed employment with the 
former employer." 
You have described a scenario in which a former employee earned overtime income but 
was not compensated for hours worked until after the fact. In a related situation, one in which a 
state employee leaves state service, but continues to "mn out" his or her leave credits, we have 
said that the applicable date for purposes of the revolving door provisions is the date of 
separation. (See e.g., Chandler Advice Letter, 1-09-032.) Similarly, here, Ms. Johnson's date of 
separation from AT&T was in 2008. She accrued income in 2005-2006 for overtime worked 
during those years, income was not received, but should have been received in the normal course 
of that employment, and she had no expectation of renewed employment by AT&T. 


File No. A-11-110 
Page No. 3 
Based on these facts, we find that the class action settlement based on work performed in 
2005-2006 is not a potentially disqualifying source of income to Ms. Johnson.2 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
HMR:jgl 
Sincerely, 
John W. Wallace 
Assistant General Counsel 
By: 
Heather M. Rowan 
Counsel, Legal Division 
2 Please note that depending on Ms. Johnson's disclosure category, she may need to report this income on 
her Statement of Economic Interests (Form 700) for the period covering 2011. (Section 87200 et seq.)

expectation of renewed employment.

QUESTION: Is the amount paid to a former employee for a class action settlement approved in 2011 
for overtime worked in 2005-2006 exempt from the definition of "income" under the Act?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Regulation 18703.3 exempts from the definition of "income" wages paid by a 
former employer that accrued before the public official became a public official if there was no 
expectation of renewed employment.
